content.text: Enterprise System Integrations

Enterprise environments present unique integration challenges: dozens of internal systems (ITSM, ERP, data warehouse, document management, identity provider), strict security and compliance requirements, rate limits and API quotas on internal platforms, and governance requirements for every action the agent takes. Rather than integrating with each system ad-hoc, the pattern is to build a thin agent integration layer — a consistent tool registry with auth, audit logging, rate limiting, and approval gates — that sits between the LLM and the underlying enterprise APIs.

attrs.alt: Enterprise agent integration hub: identity/auth, data platform, ITSM, ERP, document store, observability — all connected to the agent with an audit log

content.text: Identity and Role-Based Access

Enterprise agents must authenticate as a service principal with least-privilege access. Use OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow (not user credentials) for machine-to-machine auth, and enforce role-based tool visibility: a user can only invoke tools their AD group permits.

# pip install msal anthropic
import os
import msal  # Microsoft Authentication Library

# Azure AD app registration credentials
AAD_TENANT = os.environ["AAD_TENANT_ID"]
AAD_CLIENT_ID = os.environ["AAD_CLIENT_ID"]
AAD_CLIENT_SECRET = os.environ["AAD_CLIENT_SECRET"]
GRAPH_SCOPE = ["https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"]

_token_cache: dict = {}

def get_access_token(scope: list[str]) -> str:
    """Obtain a machine-to-machine token from Azure AD.""""
    scope_key = " ".join(scope)
    if scope_key in _token_cache and _token_cache[scope_key].get("expires_at", 0) > __import__("time").time() + 60:
        return _token_cache[scope_key]["access_token"]

    app = msal.ConfidentialClientApplication(
        client_id=AAD_CLIENT_ID,
        client_credential=AAD_CLIENT_SECRET,
        authority=f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{AAD_TENANT}",
    )
    result = app.acquire_token_for_client(scopes=scope)
    if "error" in result:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Auth error: {result['error_description']}")

    _token_cache[scope_key] = {
        "access_token": result["access_token"],
        "expires_at": __import__("time").time() + result.get("expires_in", 3600),
    }
    return result["access_token"]

def get_user_groups(user_email: str) -> list[str]:
    """Return Azure AD group names the user belongs to (for tool-level RBAC).""""
    import httpx
    token = get_access_token(GRAPH_SCOPE)
    resp = httpx.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user_email}/memberOf",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return [g.get("displayName", "") for g in resp.json().get("value", [])]

# Tool visibility filter
TOOL_PERMISSIONS = {
    "query_snowflake": ["data-analysts", "data-engineers"],
    "create_servicenow_ticket": ["support-agents", "engineers"],
    "update_sap_order": ["finance-team", "supply-chain"],
    "read_confluence_page": ["all-employees"],
}

def allowed_tools(user_groups: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    return [
        tool for tool, groups in TOOL_PERMISSIONS.items()
        if groups == ["all-employees"] or any(g in user_groups for g in groups)
    ]
Snowflake / BigQuery: Natural-Language Data Queries
A data warehouse tool lets the agent answer business questions by generating and running SQL. The agent must only generate SELECT queries (no DDL or DML) and should validate SQL before executing it.

# pip install snowflake-connector-python anthropic
import snowflake.connector, os, json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def get_snowflake_conn():
    return snowflake.connector.connect(
        user=os.environ["SNOWFLAKE_USER"],
        password=os.environ["SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD"],
        account=os.environ["SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT"],
        warehouse="COMPUTE_WH",
        database="PROD_DB",
        schema="PUBLIC",
    )

SCHEMA_CONTEXT = """
Available tables:
- orders(order_id, customer_id, amount, status, created_at)
- customers(customer_id, name, email, tier)
- products(product_id, name, category, price)
"""

def generate_and_run_sql(question: str) -> dict:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"{SCHEMA_CONTEXT}\n\n"
                f"Write a single SELECT query to answer: {question}\n"
                "Return ONLY the SQL, no explanation. "
                "Never use DROP, DELETE, UPDATE, INSERT, or CREATE."
            )
        }]
    )
    sql = resp.content[0].text.strip()

    # Safety: reject non-SELECT queries
    first_word = sql.split()[0].upper() if sql.split() else ""
    if first_word != "SELECT":
        return {"error": f"Generated non-SELECT statement: {first_word}"}

    conn = get_snowflake_conn()
    try:
        cur = conn.cursor()
        cur.execute(sql)
        rows = cur.fetchmany(100)  # Cap at 100 rows
        columns = [col[0] for col in cur.description]
        return {"sql": sql, "columns": columns, "rows": rows, "row_count": len(rows)}
    finally:
        conn.close()

# Example:
# result = generate_and_run_sql("What were the top 5 products by revenue last month?")
ServiceNow: Incident and Change Management

ServiceNow is the backbone of IT operations in most enterprises. An agent can read and create incidents, look up change requests, and update ticket fields — but write operations must go through the approval gate.

# pip install requests anthropic
import requests, os, json

SN_BASE = os.environ.get("SERVICENOW_BASE", "https://mycompany.service-now.com")
SN_USER = os.environ["SERVICENOW_USER"]
SN_PASS = os.environ["SERVICENOW_PASSWORD"]
SN_AUTH = (SN_USER, SN_PASS)

def get_active_incidents(limit: int = 10) -> list[dict]:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{SN_BASE}/api/now/table/incident",
        auth=SN_AUTH,
        params={
            "sysparm_query": "active=true^priority=1^ORpriority=2",
            "sysparm_fields": "number,short_description,priority,state,assigned_to",
            "sysparm_limit": str(limit),
        },
        headers={"Accept": "application/json"},
        timeout=15,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json().get("result", [])

def get_incident(incident_number: str) -> dict:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{SN_BASE}/api/now/table/incident",
        auth=SN_AUTH,
        params={"sysparm_query": f"number={incident_number}", "sysparm_limit": "1"},
        headers={"Accept": "application/json"},
        timeout=15,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    results = resp.json().get("result", [])
    return results[0] if results else {}

def create_incident(short_desc: str, description: str, priority: int = 3) -> dict:
    """Create a ServiceNow incident. Requires approval before calling in production.""""
    resp = requests.post(
        f"{SN_BASE}/api/now/table/incident",
        auth=SN_AUTH,
        json={
            "short_description": short_desc,
            "description": description,
            "priority": str(priority),
            "category": "software",
        },
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json", "Accept": "application/json"},
        timeout=15,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json().get("result", {})

def add_work_note(sys_id: str, note: str) -> bool:
    resp = requests.patch(
        f"{SN_BASE}/api/now/table/incident/{sys_id}",
        auth=SN_AUTH,
        json={"work_notes": note},
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        timeout=15,
    )
    return resp.status_code == 200
SharePoint / Confluence: Document Retrieval

Document management systems hold policies, runbooks, architectural decisions, and knowledge base articles. An agent with document search can answer questions like "What is the data retention policy?" or "What are the deployment steps for service X?" by retrieving the relevant document and citing it.
Enterprise Audit Logging

Every action an enterprise agent takes — every tool call, every API write, every piece of data accessed — must be logged with full context: who invoked the agent, what they asked, what tools fired, what data was returned, and whether human approval was obtained. Audit logs are the compliance and forensics record.

import json, uuid, time, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Any

logger = logging.getLogger("agent_audit")

@dataclass
class AuditEvent:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    user_id: str = ""
    session_id: str = ""
    tool_name: str = ""
    tool_inputs: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    tool_result_summary: str = ""  # Not the full result — may contain PII
    approved_by: str | None = None
    duration_ms: float = 0.0
    status: str = "ok"  # ok | error | rejected

def log_tool_call(
    user_id: str,
    session_id: str,
    tool_name: str,
    inputs: dict,
    result: Any,
    approved_by: str | None = None,
    duration_ms: float = 0.0,
) -> None:
    # Summarise the result to avoid logging sensitive data in full
    if isinstance(result, str):
        summary = result[:200]
    elif isinstance(result, (list, dict)):
        summary = f"{type(result).__name__} with {len(result)} items"
    else:
        summary = str(result)[:200]

    event = AuditEvent(
        user_id=user_id,
        session_id=session_id,
        tool_name=tool_name,
        tool_inputs={k: "***" if "password" in k.lower() or "token" in k.lower() else v
                     for k, v in inputs.items()},
        tool_result_summary=summary,
        approved_by=approved_by,
        duration_ms=duration_ms,
    )
    # Emit as structured JSON to your SIEM / logging platform
    logger.info(json.dumps(asdict(event)))
    # In production: send to Splunk, Datadog, or Azure Monitor via OTLP
Common Enterprise Integration Pitfalls
Using user credentials for the service
— storing a human's username/password for API calls breaks when the password changes and violates least-privilege. Use a service principal with its own AD app registration.
No tool-level RBAC
— if every user can invoke every tool, a help-desk agent can accidentally query financial tables. Filter the tool list per user role before passing to the LLM.
Logging full tool results
— Snowflake query results or ServiceNow incident descriptions may contain PII. Log summaries (row counts, object IDs) not full payloads.
Ignoring API rate limits
— enterprise APIs (ServiceNow, Salesforce, Graph) enforce per-minute request limits. Wrap all calls with a rate limiter that backs off gracefully rather than hammering the API.
No approval gate for writes
— an agent that can create, update, or delete records in production ERP systems without human confirmation is a liability. Require explicit approval for any mutating operation.
Summary

Enterprise agent integrations connect to identity providers (Azure AD, Okta) for service-principal auth and role-based tool filtering, data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) for natural-language SQL query execution, ITSM systems (ServiceNow, Jira) for incident and change management, and document stores (SharePoint, Confluence) for knowledge retrieval. Every tool call is audit-logged with the user, session, inputs (PII-scrubbed), and result summary. Write operations require an approval gate before execution. Rate limiting and least-privilege access are non-negotiable in regulated environments.
